## 2. Executive Summary

The April 2026 edition of the World Bank Group's *Commodity Markets Outlook* documents an unprecedented shock to global commodity markets triggered by the outbreak of war in the Middle East. The near-cessation of shipping through the **Strait of Hormuz** — a critical passageway carrying approximately 35% of global seaborne crude oil, 20% of refined petroleum products, and 20% of LNG trade — caused the **largest oil supply disruption on record**, estimated at approximately 10 million barrels per day (mb/d) in March 2026.

**Headline Findings:**

- Average commodity prices are projected to **rise 16 percent (y/y) in 2026** — the first annual increase since 2022 — and represent a 25% upward revision from January 2026 projections.
- The **Brent oil price** surged from $72/bbl (end-February) to $118/bbl (end-March), the largest monthly increase on record, before moderating into the $90s in April.
- **Energy prices** are forecast to increase 24% in 2026; the Brent oil price is projected to average **$86/bbl** for the full year.
- **Fertilizer prices** are projected to surge 31% in 2026, led by a 60% rise in urea.
- **Base metals and precious metals** prices are both projected to reach all-time highs.
- **EMDE growth** is now projected at 3.6% in 2026 (down from 4.0% expected pre-conflict), with average EMDE inflation rising to 5.1%.
- Under a risk scenario with oil above $100/bbl for a sustained period, up to **45 million additional people** could face acute food insecurity in 2026.

**Baseline Assumption:** The most acute phase of supply disruptions ends in May 2026, with Strait of Hormuz shipping volumes gradually recovering to near pre-war levels by **October 2026 (2026Q4)**.

> **Source:** Page 1, Executive Summary (full section); Table 1, Pages 12–13

## 3. Key Findings / Key Sections

| # | Finding / Section | Key Detail | Source |
|---|-------------------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | **Strait of Hormuz Closure** | Near-total halt to tanker traffic; ~35% crude oil, 20% LNG seaborne trade transits the Strait | Page 1, Executive Summary — "The state of commodity markets" |
| 2 | **Oil Supply Shock** | Reduction of ~10 mb/d in March 2026 — largest in recorded history; exceeds all prior geopolitical episodes | Page 1, Executive Summary; Figure 1.C |
| 3 | **Brent Oil Price Surge** | Rose from $72/bbl (end-Feb) to $118/bbl (end-March), +$46/bbl monthly — largest monthly increase ever recorded | Page 1–2, Executive Summary; Figure 7.C, Page 17 |
| 4 | **LNG Price Surge** | Asian LNG benchmark soared 94% in March; European natural gas rose 59%; European gas at $18/mmbtu | Page 1–2, Executive Summary |
| 5 | **Fertilizer Shock** | Fertilizer index hit post-2022 high; urea up >50% (m/m) in March; urea is epicenter of halted seaborne exports | Page 3, Executive Summary — "Outlook"; Page 34, Fertilizers section |
| 6 | **Commodity Price Forecast** | Total commodity price index projected +16% (y/y) 2026; energy +24%; fertilizers +31%; metals +17%; precious metals +42% | Page 4, Figure 3; Table 1, Pages 12–13 |
| 7 | **2027 Reversal** | Average commodity prices expected to decline 12% in 2027 as disruptions subside | Page 4, Executive Summary — Outlook |
| 8 | **EMDE Macroeconomic Impact** | EMDE growth downgraded to 3.6% (from 4%); EMDE inflation projected at 5.1% (from 4.1%); employment and development impeded | Pages 9–10, Executive Summary — "Broader implications" |
| 9 | **Food Insecurity Risk** | If oil exceeds $100/bbl for extended period: up to 45 million additional people in acute food insecurity | Page 10, Executive Summary; Figure 6.D |
| 10 | **Special Focus** | Geopolitical oil supply shocks generate 11.5% oil price increase per 1% production loss — nearly 2x prior estimates | Page 11, Executive Summary — "Special Focus"; Pages 47–56 |

## 5. Key Risks

### Upside Risks (Higher Prices)
- **More extensive/prolonged conflict disruptions** in the Middle East → Brent could average $95–$115/bbl in 2026 (Source: Page 7, Figure 5; Pages 7–8, "Risks of higher commodity prices")
- **No Strait of Hormuz reopening** until after 2026Q2 → sustained LNG shortfalls, fertilizer shortages, cascading food supply impacts (Source: Page 7)
- **Tighter base metals markets** → policy distortions, export restrictions, weather/labor disruptions at mines (Source: Page 8)
- **Prolonged sulfur supply decline** → global metals mining constrained (sulfuric acid used in ore leaching) (Source: Page 8)

### Downside Risks (Lower Prices)
- **Swift resolution of shipping disruptions** → pre-conflict oil market dynamics (near $60/bbl Brent) could reassert in 2027 (Source: Page 8)
- **Weaker-than-expected global economic growth** → tighter financial conditions, trade tensions, faltering AI investment returns, China slowdown (Source: Pages 8–9)
- **Faster EV adoption** → displaces oil demand (Source: Page 21)
- **Higher-than-expected renewable electricity generation** → displaces coal demand (Source: Page 25)

## 8. Missing or Unclear Information

| Item | Notes |
|------|-------|
| **Exact conflict parties** | The document does not specify the names of the warring parties — refers only to "war in the Middle East" and "conflict in the Gulf region" |
| **Precise damage to infrastructure** | Extent of damage to Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG facility described as "undetermined" | Source: Page 34, Fertilizers |
| **China fertilizer export restrictions** | Described as "reports that China may curb exports" — not confirmed policy at time of writing | Source: Page 34 |
| **Post-2026 outlook detail** | 2027 forecasts are presented but acknowledged to carry high uncertainty pending resolution of conflict |
| **Legal/contractual obligations** | This is an economic research report, not a legal document. No contractual clauses, penalties, or legal obligations exist within the document. |
